title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers return the indices of the two numbers that add up to a target

examples.explanation: Step 1: i=0, nums[0]=2. j=1, nums[1]=7. Check: 2+7=9 equals target 9. Found pair. Return [0, 1].
Step 1: i=0, nums[0]=3. j=1: 3+2=5 ≠ 6. j=2: 3+4=7 ≠ 6. Step 2: i=1, nums[1]=2. j=2: 2+4=6 equals target 6. Found pair. Return [1, 2].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check every possible pair of numbers in the array by using two nested loops to see if their sum equals the target. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Two Sum
ladders.desc: Complete solution using nested loops to find indices of two numbers that sum to target
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the Two Sum problem by systematically checking every possible pair of elements in the array. The intuition is straightforward: if we examine all combinations of two numbers, we're guaranteed to find the pair that sums to the target (if such a pair exists). This works because we're performing an exhaustive search through the solution space. The algorithm uses two nested loops: the outer loop selects the first element (i from 0 to n-2), and the inner loop selects the second element (j from i+1 to n-1). For each pair, we check if nums[i] + nums[j] == target. If found, we return [i, j]. If no pair is found after checking all combinations, we return an empty list or appropriate indicator. Time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n elements, we check up to n-1 other elements, resulting in n*(n-1)/2 comparisons. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, working solution. Common mistakes include: 1) Using j starting from 0 instead of i+1 (which would compare elements with themselves or duplicate pairs), 2) Not handling the case where no solution exists, 3) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries. The real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def twoSum(nums, target): n = len(nums); for i in range(n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): if nums[i] + nums[j] == target: return [i, j]; return []

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, nums[0]=2. j=1, nums[1]=7. Check: 2+7=9 equals target 9. Found pair. Return [0, 1].
Step 1: i=0, nums[0]=3. j=1: 3+2=5 ≠ 6. j=2: 3+4=7 ≠ 6. Step 2: i=1, nums[1]=2. j=2: 2+4=6 equals target 6. Found pair. Return [1, 2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pair Checking with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Implement the nested loop structure that checks all unique pairs in an array
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: generating and checking all unique pairs of elements in an array. The intuition is that to find a specific pair meeting a condition, we can systematically examine every possible combination of two elements. This works because it's a complete enumeration approach—if a solution exists, we will encounter it. The algorithm uses two loops: an outer loop with index i from 0 to n-2, and an inner loop with index j from i+1 to n-1. For each (i, j) pair, we can perform an operation (like checking if their sum equals a target). The i+1 starting point for j ensures we don't compare an element with itself and don't duplicate pairs (since (i, j) and (j, i) would be the same pair). Time complexity is O(n²) as we perform approximately n*(n-1)/2 iterations. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by providing the mechanism to examine all candidate pairs. Common mistakes: 1) Starting j at 0 (causes self-comparisons and duplicates), 2) Using j <= n instead of j < n (index out of bounds), 3) Not understanding why we need n-1 in outer loop. Real code pattern: def checkPairs(nums): n = len(nums); for i in range(n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): # Process pair (nums[i], nums[j])

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=1: pair (1,2). Step 2: i=0, j=2: pair (1,3). Step 3: i=1, j=2: pair (2,3). Total 3 pairs.
Step 1: i=0, j=1: pair (4,5). Only one pair possible.

testCases.input: nums = [a, b, c], operation: list pairs
nums = [x, y, z, w], operation: list pairs

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing Pair Sum to Target
ladders.desc: Check if the sum of two given numbers equals a target value
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the fundamental comparison operation: given two numbers a and b, and a target value, determine if a + b == target. The intuition is simple arithmetic: we're checking whether two numbers combine to exactly match a desired total. This works because addition is commutative and straightforward. The algorithm is: 1) Calculate sum = a + b, 2) Compare sum to target, 3) Return True if equal, False otherwise. Time complexity is O(1) constant time. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to the level above by being the condition checked for each pair in the nested loops. Common mistakes: 1) Using = instead of == for comparison, 2) Not considering integer overflow (though rare in typical problems), 3) Confusing the order of operations. Real code pattern: def sumEquals(a, b, target): return a + b == target

examples.trace: Step 1: Calculate 5+3=8. Step 2: Compare 8 == 8. Step 3: Result is True.
Step 1: Calculate 2+7=9. Step 2: Compare 9 == 10. Step 3: Result is False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices and understand array traversal
ladders.explanation: This building block covers how to access individual elements in an array using their indices, which is essential for the brute force approach. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element has a unique index (typically starting at 0). This works because the index serves as a direct reference to the element's position. The algorithm involves: 1) Declaring/initializing an array, 2) Using array[index] syntax to retrieve the element at that position, 3) Understanding that indices range from 0 to length-1. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for the array itself. This connects to higher levels by enabling us to retrieve nums[i] and nums[j] in the nested loops. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing (thinking first element is at index 1), 2) Accessing indices outside valid range (causing errors), 3) Confusing the element value with its index. Real code pattern: arr = [10, 20, 30]; element = arr[1] # Gets 20

examples.trace: Step 1: Array has elements at positions: index 0 → 5, index 1 → 10, index 2 → 15. Step 2: Access arr[0] returns the element at first position: 5.
Step 1: Array indices: 0→100, 1→200, 2→300. Step 2: Access arr[2] returns element at third position: 300.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Addition
ladders.desc: Add two integers together and understand basic arithmetic
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the fundamental arithmetic operation of adding two integers, which is essential for calculating the sum of pairs in Two Sum. The intuition is mathematical: addition combines two quantities into a total. This works because integers follow standard arithmetic rules. The algorithm is straightforward: given integers a and b, compute result = a + b. Time and space complexity are both O(1). This connects to higher levels by being the operation performed when checking if nums[i] + nums[j] equals target. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing addition with other operations, 2) Not handling negative numbers correctly, 3) Integer overflow in extreme cases. Real code pattern: sum = a + b

examples.trace: Step 1: Take number 3. Step 2: Add number 4. Step 3: Result is 7.
Step 1: Take number -2. Step 2: Add number 5. Step 3: Result is 3 (since -2 + 5 = 3).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Variables and Values
ladders.desc: Grasp the basic concept of variables storing values and how to assign/use them
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces variables as named containers that store values, which is prerequisite for all programming. The intuition is that variables allow us to label and reference data. This works because the programming language maintains a mapping between variable names and memory locations. The algorithm involves: 1) Declaring a variable (giving it a name), 2) Assigning a value to it, 3) Using the variable name to access its value. Time and space are not typically analyzed at this level. This connects to higher levels because variables store nums, target, indices, etc. Common mistakes: 1) Using uninitialized variables, 2) Confusing variable name with its value, 3) Not understanding that assignment copies values. Real code pattern: x = 5 (assigns value 5 to variable x)

examples.trace: Step 1: Create variable named 'num'. Step 2: Store value 10 in it. Now 'num' holds 10.

examples.input: Assign sum of a=3 and b=4 to variable 'total'
examples.trace: Step 1: Variable a=3. Step 2: Variable b=4. Step 3: Calculate 3+4=7. Step 4: Assign 7 to variable 'total'.

testCases.input: Assign sum of x=2 and y=8 to variable 'result'
Assign first element of [a,b,c] to variable 'first'
Assign product of a=6 and b=7 to variable 'product'

approaches.name: HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array once, using a hash map to store each number's index and check if the complement (target minus current number) exists in the map. This allows for fast lookups and solves the problem in linear time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Two Sum with HashMap O(n)
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding two indices where numbers sum to target using hash map for O(n) time.
ladders.explanation: The HashMap approach solves Two Sum in linear time by leveraging fast O(1) lookups. The intuition is that instead of checking every pair (O(n²)), we can store numbers we've seen and check if their complement (target - current) exists. As we iterate through the array, for each number nums[i], we calculate complement = target - nums[i]. If complement exists in our hash map, we've found our pair: the stored index of complement and current index i. If not, we store nums[i] with its index i in the map. This works because addition is commutative - if a + b = target, then when we encounter b, we'll have already stored a. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty hash map. 2) For i from 0 to n-1: a) Calculate complement = target - nums[i]. b) If complement in map: return [map[complement], i]. c) Else: map[nums[i]] = i. 3) Return empty list (though problem guarantees solution). Time: O(n) - single pass with O(1) map operations. Space: O(n) - worst case storing all elements. Connection to L1: This uses the complement checking subroutine as its core operation. Common mistakes: Returning values instead of indices, not handling duplicate numbers correctly (later occurrence overwrites earlier in map, but that's fine since we check complement before storing), assuming sorted input. Real pattern: Use dictionary/hashmap for O(1) lookups while iterating.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize map = {}. i=0, nums[0]=2, complement=9-2=7. 7 not in map. Store map[2]=0. Map = {2:0}. Step 2: i=1, nums[1]=7, complement=9-7=2. 2 in map at index 0. Return [0,1].

Step 1: map = {}. i=0, nums[0]=3, complement=6-3=3. 3 not in map. Store map[3]=0. Map = {3:0}. Step 2: i=1, nums[1]=2, complement=6-2=4. 4 not in map. Store map[2]=1. Map = {3:0, 2:1}. Step 3: i=2, nums[2]=4, complement=6-4=2. 2 in map at index 1. Return [1,2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Complement Check with HashMap
ladders.desc: Given current number and target, check if complement exists in hash map and return indices.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the HashMap solution. The intuition is that for any number x in the array, if we've previously seen its complement (target - x), we immediately have our solution. The algorithm maintains a hash map that maps numbers to their indices. For each new number: 1) Calculate complement = target - current_number. 2) Check if complement exists as a key in the hash map. 3) If yes, return [map[complement], current_index]. 4) If no, add current_number: current_index to map. This works because we're essentially 'remembering' every number we've seen and where we saw it. When we encounter a number that completes a pair with a previously seen number, we can instantly retrieve both indices. Time: O(1) per check (hash map lookup is average O(1)). Space: O(k) where k is number of elements stored so far. Connection to L0: This is the repeated operation in the main loop of the full solution. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of using complement relationships. Common mistakes: Forgetting to store the current number after checking complement (must store for future numbers), returning the complement value instead of its index, not handling the case where complement equals current number (works fine because we check before storing). Real pattern: if complement in hashmap: return [hashmap[complement], i]; else: hashmap[current] = i.

examples.trace: Step 1: complement = 13 - 11 = 2. Step 2: Check if 2 in map. Yes, at index 0. Step 3: Return [0,2]. (Don't store 11 since we found solution)
Step 1: complement = 6 - 4 = 2. Step 2: Check if 2 in map. No. Step 3: Store 4:1 in map. Map becomes {3:0, 4:1}. Step 4: Return None (continue searching)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Complement Relationship in Sum Problems
ladders.desc: Understanding that if a + b = target, then b = target - a (complement relationship).
ladders.explanation: The core mathematical insight is the complement relationship: for any two numbers a and b that sum to target, b = target - a. This transforms the problem from 'find a and b where a + b = target' to 'for each a, find if target - a exists'. This is powerful because checking for existence is faster than checking all pairs. The intuition: Instead of comparing every element with every other element (O(n²)), we can fix one element and look for its specific partner. By rearranging a + b = target to b = target - a, we know exactly what we're looking for when we encounter a. This enables using fast lookup data structures. Algorithm: For each element x in array: 1) Calculate complement = target - x. 2) Determine if complement exists in remaining array. The HashMap approach optimizes step 2 to O(1) using hash-based lookup. Time: Conceptually O(n) for the transformation. Space: No extra space needed for just the logic. Connection to L1: This logic is what the complement check subroutine implements. Connection to L3: This relies on understanding hash maps for efficient existence checking. Common mistakes: Confusing complement with negative numbers (complement = target - x works for all integers), thinking complement must be positive, not realizing this works for any target value. Real pattern: The mental model shift from 'find pairs' to 'find complement for each element'.

examples.trace: Step 1: We have a = 7. Step 2: complement = target - a = 9 - 7 = 2. Step 3: Therefore, if 7 is part of solution, we need to find 2 as the other number.
Step 1: a = -3. Step 2: complement = target - a = 5 - (-3) = 5 + 3 = 8. Step 3: So -3 pairs with 8 to make 5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Hash Map Lookup and Storage
ladders.desc: Using hash map/dictionary to store and retrieve values with O(1) average time complexity.
ladders.explanation: Hash maps (dictionaries in Python) provide average O(1) time for insertions and lookups, making them ideal for the Two Sum problem. The intuition: We need to remember numbers we've seen and quickly check if a specific number exists. Arrays would require O(n) search, but hash maps use hashing to distribute keys into buckets, allowing near-instant access. Structure: A hash map stores key-value pairs. For Two Sum, key = number, value = its index. Operations: 1) Insert: map[key] = value stores the pair. 2) Lookup: key in map checks existence. 3) Retrieve: map[key] gets the value. The hash function converts the key to an index in an underlying array. Collisions (different keys hashing to same index) are handled via chaining or open addressing, but average case remains O(1). Algorithm practice: Initialize empty map. For each number: check if complement in map (lookup), if not, store number:index (insert). Time: O(1) average per operation. Space: O(n) for storing n elements. Connection to L2: This data structure enables efficient complement checking. Connection to L4: Built upon basic key-value operations. Common mistakes: Using map before initialization, confusing key and value roles, not understanding that hash maps don't maintain order. Real pattern: dictionary = {}; dictionary[key] = value; if key in dictionary: use dictionary[key].

examples.input: Store and retrieve: numbers = [10, 20, 30] with indices
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize map = {}. Step 2: Store 10 at index 0: map[10] = 0. Map = {10:0}. Step 3: Store 20 at index 1: map[20] = 1. Map = {10:0, 20:1}. Step 4: Store 30 at index 2: map[30] = 2. Map = {10:0, 20:1, 30:2}. Step 5: Check if 20 in map: True, retrieve map[20] = 1.

examples.input: Check nonexistent key: map = {5:0, 8:1}, check for 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Map contains keys 5 and 8. Step 2: Check '3 in map'. Step 3: Hash function computes index for 3. Step 4: At that index, no key 3 found (or collision chain doesn't contain 3). Step 5: Return False.

testCases.input: map = {a:0, b:1}, retrieve value for key='a'
map = {}, check if any key exists

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Iteration with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Looping through an array while keeping track of current index and element value.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves traversing each element of an array sequentially while knowing both the value and its position (index). The intuition: Many array problems require examining each element, and often we need the index for the solution (like Two Sum which returns indices). Basic pattern: Use a for-loop with index variable i that goes from 0 to length-1. At each iteration, we access the current element as array[i]. In Python, enumerate() combines both: for i, num in enumerate(nums): gives index i and value num. Algorithm: 1) Get array length n. 2) Initialize i = 0. 3) While i < n: a) current_value = array[i]. b) Process current_value with index i. c) i += 1. Time: O(n) for traversing n elements. Space: O(1) for index variable. Connection to L3: Hash map operations occur inside this iteration. Connection to L5: Built upon understanding arrays and loops. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (using <= instead of <), modifying array while iterating, forgetting to increment index in while-loop. Real pattern: for i in range(len(array)): value = array[i] or for i, value in enumerate(array):.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 3. i=0, array[0]=10, print '0:10'. Step 2: i=1, array[1]=20, print '1:20'. Step 3: i=2, array[2]=30, print '2:30'. Step 4: i=3, stop (3 not < 3).
Step 1: Initialize sum=0. Step 2: i=0, array[0]=5, sum=0+5=5. Step 3: i=1, array[1]=1, sum=5+1=6. Step 4: i=2, array[2]=9, sum=6+9=15. Step 5: i=3, stop. Return sum=15.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays as indexed collections and performing subtraction.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers two prerequisites: 1) Arrays: Ordered collections where each element has an index (position) starting at 0. Arrays allow random access: given index i, we can get element array[i]. Example: array = [2,7,11,15] has array[0]=2, array[1]=7, etc. 2) Basic arithmetic: Subtraction operation. For Two Sum, we need target - current_number. Subtraction is inverse of addition: if a + b = target, then target - a = b. Intuition: Arrays store our numbers, and subtraction helps us find what we're looking for. Practice: Access elements by index, compute differences. Algorithm: Access: value = array[index]. Subtract: complement = target - value. Time: O(1) for access and arithmetic. Space: O(1) for variables. Connection to L4: This enables the iteration where we access each element and compute its complement. Common mistakes: Thinking indices start at 1, confusing array values with indices, arithmetic errors with negative numbers. Real pattern: Access: nums[i]. Compute: target - nums[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: array[1] = 10. Step 2: target = 20. Step 3: complement = 20 - 10 = 10.
Step 1: array[0] = 8. Step 2: target = 8. Step 3: complement = 8 - 8 = 0.

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n log n)
approaches.summary: First sort the array with indices tracked, then use two pointers (one at start, one at end) to find the pair that sums to the target by adjusting pointers based on the sum. This is efficient but requires sorting and index handling.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Two Sum with Two Pointer Approach
ladders.desc: Complete solution using sorting with index tracking and two-pointer technique to find indices of two numbers summing to target.
ladders.explanation: The two-pointer approach for Two Sum transforms the problem into a sorted search problem. First, we create an array of tuples (value, original_index) to preserve indices after sorting. Sorting this array by value takes O(n log n) time. Then we initialize two pointers: left at index 0 and right at index n-1. While left < right, we calculate current_sum = sorted[left].value + sorted[right].value. If current_sum equals target, we return [sorted[left].index, sorted[right].index]. If current_sum < target, we increment left pointer to increase the sum (since array is sorted ascending). If current_sum > target, we decrement right pointer to decrease the sum. This works because sorting allows us to systematically explore pairs from both ends, guaranteeing we'll find the solution if it exists. The time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting plus O(n) for the two-pointer scan, dominated by O(n log n). Space complexity is O(n) for storing the indexed array. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle duplicate indices (not allowed), not preserving original indices properly, and incorrect pointer movement logic. This approach is particularly useful when you need to return values rather than indices, or when the array can be modified.

examples.trace:
1. Create indexed array: [(2,0), (7,1), (11,2), (15,3)]
2. Sort by value: [(2,0), (7,1), (11,2), (15,3)] (already sorted)
3. Initialize left=0, right=3
4. current_sum = 2+15=17 > 9, so right=2
5. current_sum = 2+11=13 > 9, so right=1
6. current_sum = 2+7=9 == target, return [0,1]

1. Create indexed array: [(3,0), (2,1), (4,2)]
2. Sort by value: [(2,1), (3,0), (4,2)]
3. Initialize left=0, right=2
4. current_sum = 2+4=6 == target, return [1,2]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Two Pointer Search on Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Given a sorted array of numbers, find if any pair sums to target using two pointers.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core algorithmic component after sorting. The intuition is that in a sorted array, we can efficiently search for pairs by starting with the smallest and largest elements. If their sum is too small, we need a larger sum, so we move the left pointer rightward. If their sum is too large, we need a smaller sum, so we move the right pointer leftward. This works because the array is sorted in ascending order: moving left pointer right increases the smallest element in consideration, moving right pointer left decreases the largest element. The algorithm maintains the invariant that all pairs with indices between left and right are still candidates. We continue until left >= right or we find the target sum. Time complexity is O(n) for the pointer traversal. Space complexity is O(1) excluding input storage. Common mistakes include moving wrong pointer (should move left when sum < target, right when sum > target), not handling the case where no solution exists, and off-by-one errors in pointer updates. This sub-routine feeds into L0 by being the search mechanism after the array is properly sorted with indices tracked.

examples.trace:
1. left=0 (value=1), right=4 (value=5)
2. sum=1+5=6 < 7, move left=1
3. sum=2+5=7 == target, return values [2,5]

1. left=0 (10), right=3 (40)
2. sum=10+40=50 == target, return values [10,40]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pointer Movement Based on Sum Comparison
ladders.desc: The decision logic for moving pointers when current sum doesn't equal target.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental insight that makes two-pointer search work. When we have two pointers left and right pointing to elements in a sorted array, and their sum doesn't equal the target, we must decide which pointer to move. The key intuition: if sum < target, we need a larger sum. Since the array is sorted ascending, moving left pointer rightward increases the left element (making it larger), thus potentially increasing the sum. Moving right pointer leftward would decrease the right element (making it smaller), which would decrease the sum - the opposite of what we need. Conversely, if sum > target, we need a smaller sum, so we move right pointer leftward to decrease the right element. This systematic adjustment guarantees we explore all relevant pairs without missing any. The algorithm essentially performs a constrained binary search in two dimensions. Time complexity for this logic per comparison is O(1). Space is O(1). Common mistakes include moving the wrong pointer (e.g., moving right when sum < target), which could skip valid pairs, or moving both pointers simultaneously, which could miss solutions. This logic is the engine of the L1 sub-routine, enabling efficient search through the sorted array.

examples.trace:
1. current_sum = 2+8=10
2. Compare: 10 > target 7
3. Need smaller sum, so move right pointer left to get smaller right_value

1. current_sum = 3+5=8
2. Compare: 8 < target 9
3. Need larger sum, so move left pointer right to get larger left_value

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting with Index Preservation
ladders.desc: Sort an array while keeping track of original indices for later retrieval.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is crucial because the two-pointer approach requires a sorted array to work, but we need to return original indices, not sorted positions. The solution is to create an array of tuples (value, original_index) before sorting. We sort this array based on the value component. After sorting, we have the values in ascending order with their original indices attached. This allows us to perform the two-pointer search on sorted values, then retrieve the original indices for the final answer. The intuition is that we're decorating each value with metadata (its original position) before sorting, then using that metadata after sorting. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting n tuples. Space complexity is O(n) for the new array. Common mistakes include forgetting to store indices before sorting, incorrectly implementing the sort key (should sort by value only), or confusing sorted indices with original indices in the final step. This building block enables L1 and L0 by providing the properly structured sorted data that the two-pointer algorithm needs while maintaining the ability to return correct indices.

examples.trace:
1. Create list of tuples: [(3,0), (1,1), (2,2)]
2. Sort by first element (value): compare 3,1,2
3. Sorted order: 1,2,3 with indices 1,2,0 respectively

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Sums and Making Decisions
ladders.desc: Compare the sum of two numbers to a target and determine next action.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the atomic decision unit in the two-pointer algorithm. Given three numbers: left_value, right_value, and target, we compute sum = left_value + right_value and compare it to target. There are three possible outcomes: 1) sum == target: we found the solution. 2) sum < target: we need a larger sum. 3) sum > target: we need a smaller sum. The intuition is simple arithmetic: if our current sum is less than desired, we need to increase it; if greater, we need to decrease it. In the context of sorted arrays with two pointers, this dictates which pointer to move. This operation has O(1) time complexity and O(1) space. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison operators (e.g., sum <= target instead of sum < target), which could cause incorrect pointer movement, or forgetting to handle the equal case separately. This basic operation is repeated in L2's core logic and forms the essential comparison step that drives the entire two-pointer search algorithm.

examples.trace:
1. sum = 3+5=8
2. Compare: 8 == 8
3. Result: found solution

1. sum = 2+7=9
2. Compare: 9 > 6
3. Result: sum too large, need smaller sum

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Addition and Comparison of Numbers
ladders.desc: Basic arithmetic: adding two numbers and comparing the result to a third number.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept needed for the entire Two Sum problem. It involves two fundamental operations: 1) Addition: combining two numbers to get their sum. For integers a and b, sum = a + b. 2) Comparison: determining the relationship between the sum and a target number using comparison operators (==, <, >). If sum equals target, they're equal. If sum is less than target, the sum is smaller. If sum is greater than target, the sum is larger. The intuition is that we're checking whether two numbers together reach a specific total. This is literally the core of the Two Sum problem statement: find two numbers that add up to a target. Time and space complexity are both O(1) for these basic operations. Common mistakes include integer overflow (not relevant for typical constraints), incorrect order of operations, or misunderstanding comparison operators. This foundation is used in L4's basic operation every time we check a pair of numbers, and ultimately throughout the entire algorithm to determine when we've found the solution or which direction to search next.

examples.trace:
1. Add: 2+3=5
2. Compare: 5 == 5
3. Result: sum equals target

1. Add: 4+5=9
2. Compare: 9 > 8
3. Result: sum greater than target
